preamble: The diphthong 'uo' historically transformed into 'ue' in the development of several Romance languages, notably Old French and Old Spanish. This shift typically involved a change in the second vowel element, where the back, rounded 'o' became a front, unrounded 'e'. This process, often linked to the diphthongization of Vulgar Latin open-mid vowels, represents a significant sound change in the evolution of these languages.
phoneticEffects: Vowel fronting, unrounding, diphthongization, often stemming from tonic vowel breaking
sources: Limanni, A. (2008). From uo to ue in Spanish and from uo to o in Sicilian: Same Problem, Different Solutions. In Selected Proceedings of the 3rd Conference on Laboratory Approaches to Spanish Phonology, 125-139. Cascadilla Proceedings Project.
The Phonetic Value of Old French Ue from Vulgar Latin Open O. (2020). PMLA, 45(2).
